entries.title: What a vhost is and why to brute-force the Host header
entries.body:
A single IP can host many domains/subdomains (Apache/Nginx/IIS) — the server selects the site by the Host header.
Default requests only hit the main vhost; HIDDEN hosts may expose extra functionality or vulnerabilities.
Idea: supply different Host values and compare the responses.

entries.title: How Host drives routing
entries.body:
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: site-a.com

--- different Host -> different content ---

GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: site-b.com

entries.title: Signs that you hit a different vhost
entries.body:
• Different <title>, meta description, brand
• Different Content-Length / body size
• Different status code (200 vs 403 / redirect)
• Custom error pages
• Redirect chains to a completely different domain
• A certificate with different domains in SAN

entries.title: hakoriginfinder — find the origin behind a proxy/WAF
DNS history -> real IP
entries.body: Use DNS history to find the domain's old IPs. Then "spray" the current domains across those IPs. A successful response = the real origin -> you can bypass Cloudflare/WAF by hitting the server directly.

entries.title: Tools
entries.body:
VhostFinder — search by response similarity.
VHostScan — detect catch-all, aliases, dynamic default pages.
hakoriginfinder — origin behind a reverse proxy.
gobuster vhost — brute-force.
